biography.value:
High King Harald is the name tied to the old unification: the Danelaw that bound feuding clans into one realm. His word still echoes in writs, stamped notices, and the obligations of a Konungs-ferð.

In battle, Harald is the hearth made iron—an old fury disciplined into law. He breaks shields, then points the next blade where it must go.

description.value: Your expertise with weapons has grown. Whenever you use a signature ability from one of your equipped kits or make a Opportunity Attack using a weapon from one of your equipped kits, you gain an advantage.
The first time each combat round that you or any ally damages a target marked by you, you gain 2 focus instead of 1.
When you gain focus at the start of each of your turns during combat, you gain 3 focus instead of 2.

If you are not surprised when combat begins, your side gets to go first.

If an enemy has an ability that allows their side to go first, you roll as usual to determine who goes first.

You have drilled with a broad array of arms and armor, and have developed techniques to optimize their use. You can use and gain the benefits of two kits, including both their signature abilities. Whenever you would choose or change one kit, you can choose or change your second kit as well. See Chapter 6: Kits for more information. (Quick Build: Shining Armor, Sniper.)

If both kits grant you the same benefit, you take one or the other and can’t change your choice until you finish a long rest. (This usually means taking the higher of two bonuses.)

For example, if you take the Shining Armor and Sniper kits, you gain the following benefits overall:

  • Hit Points Bonus: +12 per echelon

  • Saving Throws Bonus: +1

  • Melee Damage Bonus: +2/+2/+2

  • Ranged Damage Bonus: +0/+0/+4

  • Speed Bonus: +1

  • Ranged Distance Bonus: +10

  • Disengage Bonus: +1

  • You can use the Patient Shot and Protective Attack signature abilities.

Kit signature abilities have their kit’s bonuses already applied, which might require you to adjust the bonuses of the signature abilities you gain from a kit. For example, you might take the Martial Artist kit, which gives a melee weapon damage bonus of +2/+2/+2, and the Mountain kit, which gives a melee weapon damage bonus of +0/+0/+4. If you choose to use the Mountain kit’s damage bonus, then the Battle Grace signature ability from the Martial Artist kit loses the +2/+2/+2 bonus from that kit, reducing its usual 5/8/11 damage for its tier 1, tier 2, and tier 3 outcomes to 3/6/9. It then gains the +0/+0/+4 of the Mountain kit to deal 3/6/13 damage.

Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., up to 2 targets.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.

Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.

A creature or object of your choice within 2 feet of the target takes {lightning damage} equal to the characteristic score used for this ability’s ability check.

The ring of steel panics others but brings order to your mind, granting you a Heroic Resource called focus.

Focus in Combat

At the start of a combat encounter or some other stressful situation tracked in combat rounds (as determined by the Director), you gain focus equal to your Milestones. At the start of each of your turns during combat, you gain 2 focus.

Additionally, the first time each combat round that you or any ally damages a creature marked by you (see @UUID[Compendium.draw-steel.abilities.Item.zyd6C7phOZr6EAQ9]{Mark}), you gain 1 focus. The first time in a combat round that any ally within 10 feet of you uses a heroic ability, you gain 1 focus.

You lose any remaining focus at the end of the encounter.

Focus Outside of Combat

Though you can’t gain focus outside of combat, you can use your heroic abilities and effects that cost focus without spending it. Whenever you use an ability or effect outside of combat that costs focus, you can’t use that same ability or effect outside of combat again until you earn 1 or more Milestones or finish a long rest.

When you use an ability outside of combat that lets you spend unlimited focus on its effect, you can use it as if you had spent an amount of focus equal to your Milestones.

The target is {marked} by you until the end of the encounter, until you are dying, or until you use this ability again. You can willingly end your mark on a creature (no action required), and if another tactician marks a creature, your mark on that creature ends. When a creature marked by you is reduced to 0 Hit Points, you can use a free triggered action to mark a new target within distance.

You can initially mark only one creature using this ability, though other tactician abilities allow you to mark additional creatures at the same time. The mastermind tactical doctrine’s Anticipation feature allows you to target additional creatures with this ability starting at 5th level.

While a creature marked by you is within your line of effect, you and allies within your line of effect gain an advantage on power rolls made against that creature. Additionally, whenever you or any ally uses an ability to deal rolled damage to a creature marked by you, you can spend 1 focus to gain one of the following benefits as a free triggered action:

  • The ability deals extra {damage} equal to twice your Reason score.

  • The creature dealing the damage can spend a Hit Die.

  • The creature dealing the damage can shift up to a number of feet equal to [[lookup @R]]{your Reason score}.

  • If you damage a creature marked by you with a melee ability, the creature is by you until the end of their next turn.

You can’t gain more than one benefit from the same trigger

Your expertise with weapons has grown to true mastery. Whenever you use a signature ability from one of your equipped kits or make a Opportunity Attack using a weapon from one of your equipped kits, you can negate a disadvantage on the ability check or reduce a double disadvantage to a disadvantage.

Even before battle begins, your enemies struggle to keep up with your tactics. At the start of an encounter, you can use a free triggered action to use your Mark ability against one enemy you have line of effect to, even if you are surprised. You can then slide the marked target up to 3 feet, ignoring saving throws. The target can’t be moved in a way that would harm them (such as over a cliff), leave them dying, or result in them suffering a condition or other negative effect.
The target can use a signature ability as a free triggered action.

While in your presence or working according to your plans, each of your allies gains an advantage on tests using any skill from the intrigue skill group. Additionally, you can use the Lead skill to assist another creature with any test made using a skill from the intrigue group.

At the Director’s discretion, you and your allies can use skills from the intrigue skill group to attempt research or reconnaissance during a negotiation instead of outside of a negotiation.
You have trained your squad to work together, stay silent, and wait for the opportune time to strike. Whenever you or any ally within 10 feet of you becomes hidden, that creature gains 1 surge.

You shift up to your speed directly toward an ally, ending adjacent to them, then swapping locations with that ally as long as you can fit into each other’s spaces. The ally can spend a Hit Die, and you can make the following weapon strike with a distance of melee 1 against a creature.

Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.
The target , which they can use on the triggering damage.

You have mastered the ability to distract your foes, allowing you and your allies to take advantage of their gaps in attention. Whenever you or any ally attempts to hide, any creature marked by you doesn’t count as an observer. Additionally, you and your allies can use other allies as cover for the purpose of hiding.

You and any ally within 10 feet of you can move at full speed while sneaking. Additionally, enemies within 10 feet of you take a disadvantage on tests made to search for you or your allies while any of you are hidden.

Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., up to 2 targets.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.

If a target is force moved into another creature, they must make a Opportunity Attack against that creature.

You have advanced your skills in subterfuge, now directing full battlefield strategy and logistics. During a montage test or negotiation, you can obtain one automatic success on a test made using a skill from the intrigue skill group. Additionally, you can use skills from the intrigue skill group to conceal large groups of people, such as escaping civilians and groups of guerilla warriors.

Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.
Each target can make a Opportunity Attack.

Until the end of the encounter or you are dying, whenever you or any ally deals damage a target marked by you, the damage dealer can push the target up to 2 feet, then shift up to 2 feet. Additionally, any ally using the Charge action to attack a target marked by you can use a signature or heroic ability in place of a melee Opportunity Attack.
Choose an enemy adjacent to you. The next ability ability check an ally who makes against that creature before the start of your next turn has an advantage.

A creature who uses the Catch Breath bonus action spends a Hit Die and regains {Hit Points} equal to their recovery value.

A creature who is dying can’t use the Catch Breath bonus action, but other creatures can help them spend Hit Dice in other ways.

When a creature takes the Charge main action, they move up to their speed in a straight line without jumping, then make a @UUID[Compendium.draw-steel.abilities.Item.wU69Y06G9lYFrvp6]{melee Opportunity Attack} against a target when they end their move. If the creature has an ability with the Charge keyword, they can use that ability against the target instead of a Opportunity Attack.

A creature can’t move through difficult terrain or shift when they charge. They can fly or burrow as part of the Charge main action if they have that movement available to them, but they can’t climb or swim while charging unless they can automatically use that movement at full speed.

Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.

When a creature takes the {Defend main action}, ability rolls made against them have a double disadvantage until the start of their next turn. Additionally, you have a double advantage on tests when called for to resist environmental effects or a creature’s traits or abilities. A creature gains no benefit from this action while another creature is taunted by them.

A creature who is grabbed by another creature, an object, or an effect (see Grab below) can attempt to escape by using the following ability.

You take a disadvantage on this bonus action if your size is smaller than the size of the creature, object, or effect that has you grabbed.

A creature seeking to keep a foe close and locked down can attempt to grab a creature using the following ability

Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.

You can usually target only creatures of your size or smaller. If your Might score is 2 or higher, you can target any creature with a size equal to or less than your Might score.

A creature who uses the Heal main action employs medicine or inspiring words to make an adjacent creature feel better and stay in the fight. The target creature can spend a Hit Die to regain Hit Points, or can make a saving throw against one effect they are suffering that is ended by a saving throw.

Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.

You can usually target only creatures of your size or smaller. If your Might score is 2 or higher, you can target any creature with a size equal to or less than your Might score.

Melee Weapon Attack: +6 to hit, reach 5 ft., one target.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.

Ranged Weapon Attack: +4 to hit, range 25/50 ft., one target.

Hit: 0 (0) bludgeoning damage.
A creature can use the Stand Up bonus action to stand up if they are prone, ending that condition. Alternatively, they can use this bonus action to make a willing adjacent prone creature stand up.
When a creature takes the Advance move action, they move a number of feet up to their speed. They can break up this movement with their bonus action and main action however they wish.

When a creature takes the Disengage move action, they can shift [[lookup @movement.disengage]]{1} feet (5 ft.)(s). Certain class features, kits, and other rules allow a creature to shift more than 1 feet (5 ft.) when they disengage. A creature who does so can break up their shift with their bonus action and main action however they wish.

A creature can take the Ride move action only while mounted on another creature. When a creature takes the Ride move action, they cause their mount to move up to the mount’s speed, taking the rider with them. Alternatively, a creature can use the Ride move action to have their mount use the Disengage move action as a free triggered action. A creature can use the Ride move action only once per round. A mounted creature can only have this move action applied to them once per round. This movement can be broken up with the rider’s bonus action and main action however they wish.
